event: The Myrrh-Bearing Women at the Tomb
symbols: women at the tomb — the faithful disciples who came to anoint Jesus's body
seated grieving figure — the weight of sorrow before the discovery of the empty tomb
standing veiled figure — the pilgrimage to the place of death that will become the place of life
dawn light on the horizon — the first light of Easter morning breaking through darkness
dark landscape — the gloom of death about to be dispelled by resurrection
description: A somber landscape scene at dawn or dusk. A young woman in a brown robe sits on the ground near what appears to be a rocky area or garden, with a small vessel beside her. She hugs her knees in a posture of grief. Behind her, a taller figure in a dark veil stands, looking into the distance. Additional shadowy figures are barely visible in the background. Trees and vegetation frame the left side. The sky shows a pale band of light at the horizon. The overall mood is deeply melancholic and contemplative.
theological_description: Bashkirtseff's painting depicts the myrrh-bearing women — the faithful female disciples who came to anoint Jesus's body on Easter morning. Mark records: 'When the Sabbath was past, Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James, and Salome bought spices, so that they might go and anoint him. And very early on the first day of the week, when the sun had risen, they went to the tomb' (Mark 16:1-2). These women demonstrated a devotion that surpassed the male disciples — they alone came to the tomb while the apostles hid in fear. Luke notes they 'had followed him from Galilee' and 'saw the tomb and how his body was laid. Then they returned and prepared spices and ointments' (Luke 23:55-56). The painting captures the poignant moment of grief before the stunning discovery of the empty tomb and the angel's proclamation: 'He is not here, for he has risen' (Matthew 28:6). Their faithfulness in the darkness of Holy Saturday, before any knowledge of the resurrection, embodies the trust of those who serve God without seeing the outcome. As Jesus would later say to Thomas: 'Blessed are those who have not seen and yet have believed' (John 20:29).

scripture_refs.reason: The women bringing spices to anoint Jesus's body early on Easter morning — the direct scene
The women who followed from Galilee preparing spices and ointments — their faithful devotion
The angel's announcement that Jesus has risen — the discovery these women are approaching
Blessed are those who believe without seeing — the women's faith in darkness before the resurrection
